content:
You are a programming expert. 
You will be provided with a patch used to fix a bug. 

Your task is summarize the modifications made in the patch.
Your summary should consist of two parts, modifications in each file and their relationships.

From ea30e11970a96cfe5e32c03a29332554573b4a10 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: Neil Horman <nhorman@tuxdriver.com>
Date: Tue, 2 Jun 2009 01:29:58 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] e1000: add missing length check to e1000 receive routine

	Patch to fix bad length checking in e1000.  E1000 by default does two
things:

1) Spans rx descriptors for packets that don't fit into 1 skb on recieve
2) Strips the crc from a frame by subtracting 4 bytes from the length prior to
doing an skb_put

Since the e1000 driver isn't written to support receiving packets that span
multiple rx buffers, it checks the End of Packet bit of every frame, and
discards it if its not set.  This places us in a situation where, if we have a
spanning packet, the first part is discarded, but the second part is not (since
it is the end of packet, and it passes the EOP bit test).  If the second part of
the frame is small (4 bytes or less), we subtract 4 from it to remove its crc,
underflow the length, and wind up in skb_over_panic, when we try to skb_put a
huge number of bytes into the skb.  This amounts to a remote DOS attack through
careful selection of frame size in relation to interface MTU.  The fix for this
is already in the e1000e driver, as well as the e1000 sourceforge driver, but no
one ever pushed it to e1000.  This is lifted straight from e1000e, and prevents
small frames from causing the underflow described above

Signed-off-by: Neil Horman <nhorman@tuxdriver.com>
Tested-by: Andy Gospodarek <andy@greyhouse.net>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>

diff --git a/drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c b/drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c
index b1419e21b46b..fffb006b7d95 100644
--- a/drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c
+++ b/drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c
@@ -4027,8 +4027,9 @@ static bool e1000_clean_rx_irq(struct e1000_adapter *adapter,
 		                 PCI_DMA_FROMDEVICE);
 
 		length = le16_to_cpu(rx_desc->length);
-
-		if (unlikely(!(status & E1000_RXD_STAT_EOP))) {
+		/* !EOP means multiple descriptors were used to store a single
+		 * packet, also make sure the frame isn't just CRC only */
+		if (unlikely(!(status & E1000_RXD_STAT_EOP) || (length <= 4))) {
 			/* All receives must fit into a single buffer */
 			E1000_DBG("%s: Receive packet consumed multiple"
 				  " buffers\n", netdev->name);
-- 
2.34.1

### Modifications in Each File

**File: `drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c`**
- A modification was made in the `e1000_clean_rx_irq` function.
- The condition that checks if the End of Packet (EOP) bit is set has been updated. The new condition now also checks if the length of the received packet is less than or equal to 4 bytes.
- The updated condition is: 
  ```c
  if (unlikely(!(status & E1000_RXD_STAT_EOP) || (length <= 4))) {
  ```
- This change ensures that packets that either do not have the EOP bit set or are small enough (4 bytes or less) are properly handled, preventing potential underflow issues when processing the packet.

### Relationships Between Modifications
- The modification in `e1000_main.c` addresses a specific bug related to the handling of packets that span multiple receive descriptors and those that are small enough to only contain a CRC. 
- By adding the additional length check, the patch prevents a scenario where a small packet could lead to an underflow when the CRC is stripped, which could cause a denial of service (DoS) vulnerability.
- This fix aligns the behavior of the e1000 driver with that of the e1000e driver, which already implemented similar checks, thereby improving the robustness and security of the e1000 driver.

Based on your summary and the commit message in the patch, you need to:

1. analyze the root cause of the bug in detail. 
2. output the file name that is most likely to cause the bug. 



Provide a response strictly in the following format:
Root cause:<Root cause>
file:<file name>

Based on your summary and the commit message in the patch, you need to:

1. analyze the root cause of the bug in detail. 
2. output the file name that is most likely to cause the bug. 



Provide a response strictly in the following format:
Root cause:<Root cause>
file:<file name>
You are a programming expert.

The code statements that lead to the bug are:
```c
if (unlikely(!(status & E1000_RXD_STAT_EOP))) {
```
```c
length -= 4;
```
```c
skb_put(skb, length);
```
Reason:
The bug occurs because the e1000 driver does not handle packets that span multiple receive descriptors correctly. When a packet is split, the first part may be discarded if it does not have the End of Packet (EOP) bit set, while the second part, which does have the EOP bit set, is processed. If this second part is small (4 bytes or less), subtracting the CRC from its length can lead to an underflow, causing a panic when attempting to add a large number of bytes to the socket buffer (skb).

The code statements that fix the bug are:
```c
if (unlikely(!(status & E1000_RXD_STAT_EOP) || (length <= 4))) {
```
```c
buffer_info->skb = skb;
```
Reason:
The fix adds a check to ensure that if the packet does not have the EOP bit set or if its length is 4 bytes or less, the skb is recycled instead of processed. This prevents the underflow condition that occurs when subtracting the CRC from a small packet length, thus avoiding the panic and potential denial of service vulnerability.

If this second part is small (4 bytes or less), subtracting the CRC from its length can lead to an underflow, causing a panic when attempting to add a large number of bytes to the socket buffer (skb).

The code statements that fix the bug are:
```c

```
```c
buffer_info->skb = skb;
```
Reason:
The fix adds a check to ensure that if the packet does not have the EOP bit set or if its length is 4 bytes or less, the skb is recycled instead of processed. This prevents the underflow condition that occurs when subtracting the CRC from a small packet length, thus avoiding the panic and potential denial of service vulnerability.

content: You are a programming expert.

Reason: The provided code snippet contains the original bug because it does not include the additional check for the length of the packet after the EOP check. Specifically, it only checks if the EOP bit is not set, but it does not account for the case where the length is 4 bytes or less, which could lead to an underflow when subtracting the CRC. This oversight can still result in a panic and potential denial of service vulnerability.

Result: yes
You are a programming expert.

Reason: The provided code snippet contains the original bug because it includes the line `length -= 4;` without the necessary checks to prevent underflow when the length is 4 bytes or less. Although the initial condition checks for the End of Packet (EOP) bit and the length, the subsequent adjustment of `length` does not account for the possibility of underflow, which can lead to a panic. Therefore, the code is still vulnerable to the described denial of service attack.

Result: yes

begin to deal with drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c in strategy1

You will be given the root cause of a bug, the commit message and the corresponding patch.
        You need to extract all code statements that lead to the bug from the patch, based on the root cause and the commit message.

        Root cause:
        The bug arises from the e1000 driver's inability to handle packets that span multiple receive descriptors correctly. When a packet is split across descriptors, the first part may be discarded if it does not have the End of Packet (EOP) bit set, while the second part, which does have the EOP bit set, is processed. If this second part is small (4 bytes or less), subtracting the CRC from its length can lead to an underflow, causing a panic when attempting to add a large number of bytes to the socket buffer (skb). This vulnerability can be exploited to create a denial of service (DoS) attack.
        
        Commit message:
        e1000: add missing length check to e1000 receive routine

	Patch to fix bad length checking in e1000.  E1000 by default does two
things:

1) Spans rx descriptors for packets that don't fit into 1 skb on recieve
2) Strips the crc from a frame by subtracting 4 bytes from the length prior to
doing an skb_put

Since the e1000 driver isn't written to support receiving packets that span
multiple rx buffers, it checks the End of Packet bit of every frame, and
discards it if its not set.  This places us in a situation where, if we have a
spanning packet, the first part is discarded, but the second part is not (since
it is the end of packet, and it passes the EOP bit test).  If the second part of
the frame is small (4 bytes or less), we subtract 4 from it to remove its crc,
underflow the length, and wind up in skb_over_panic, when we try to skb_put a
huge number of bytes into the skb.  This amounts to a remote DOS attack through
careful selection of frame size in relation to interface MTU.  The fix for this
is already in the e1000e driver, as well as the e1000 sourceforge driver, but no
one ever pushed it to e1000.  This is lifted straight from e1000e, and prevents
small frames from causing the underflow described above

Signed-off-by: Neil Horman <nhorman@tuxdriver.com>
Tested-by: Andy Gospodarek <andy@greyhouse.net>
Signed-off-by: David S. Miller <davem@davemloft.net>  
        
        Patch:
        diff --git a/drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c b/drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c
index b1419e21b46b..fffb006b7d95 100644
--- a/drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c
+++ b/drivers/net/e1000/e1000_main.c
@@ -4027,8 +4027,9 @@ static bool e1000_clean_rx_irq(struct e1000_adapter *adapter,
 		                 PCI_DMA_FROMDEVICE);
 
 		length = le16_to_cpu(rx_desc->length);
-
-		if (unlikely(!(status & E1000_RXD_STAT_EOP))) {
+		/* !EOP means multiple descriptors were used to store a single
+		 * packet, also make sure the frame isn't just CRC only */
+		if (unlikely(!(status & E1000_RXD_STAT_EOP) || (length <= 4))) {
 			/* All receives must fit into a single buffer */
 			E1000_DBG("%s: Receive packet consumed multiple"
 				  " buffers\n", netdev->name);
--
